title: Observation of the Magnus Nonlinear Hall effect from Chiral Weyl Monopoles
authors: Heda Zhang, Nikolai Peshcherenko, Ning Mao, Nianlong Zou, Jiaqiang Yan, Claudia Felser, Yang Zhang
abstract: The nonlinear Hall effect (NLHE) connects crystalline symmetry to quantum geometry, offering a probe of band topology beyond linear transport. While most studies have focused on the Berry curvature dipole in low-symmetry crystals, mechanisms that directly probe Berry monopoles in higher-symmetry chiral lattices remain unexplored. Here, we report the observations of the NLHE in the chiral Weyl semimetal CoSi, a platform where the Berry curvature dipole is symmetry-forbidden. By employing focused ion beam-fabricated crossbar devices, we detect a robust second-harmonic Hall voltage under zero magnetic field, hosting all key signatures of the NLHE. Theoretical analysis attributes the nonlinear Hall conductivity to skew scattering of self-rotating electron wave packets, whose chirality is dictated by the underlying band topology, a process reminiscent of the classical Magnus effect. Furthermore, the NLHE signal exhibits a temperature-dependent sign reversal, and a strong, linearly field-dependent modulation that grows with carrier mobility, directly reflecting the topological Weyl nodes distribution near the Fermi level. These findings establish CoSi as a platform for Berry monopole-driven nonlinear transport, demonstrating a skew-scattering route to topological nonlinear Hall responses that bypasses conventional symmetry constraints.
summary: The researchers observed a nonlinear Hall effect in the chiral Weyl semimetal CoSi, even though the standard Berry curvature dipole is forbidden by symmetry. They found that the effect arises from the skew scattering of electron wave packets due to Berry monopoles, providing a new way to probe band topology through nonlinear transport.
main_problem: Exploring mechanisms for the nonlinear Hall effect (NLHE) in high-symmetry chiral lattices where the Berry curvature dipole is symmetry-forbidden.
main_result: Observation of a robust NLHE in the chiral Weyl semimetal CoSi, attributed to the skew scattering of self-rotating electron wave packets driven by Berry monopoles.
method: Experimental detection using focused ion beam-fabricated crossbar devices combined with theoretical analysis of skew scattering and topological wave packet dynamics.
model_or_system: The chiral Weyl semimetal CoSi, characterized by its high-symmetry chiral lattice and the presence of topological Weyl nodes.
key_observables: Second-harmonic Hall voltage, temperature-dependent sign reversal, and linearly field-dependent modulation of the NLHE signal.
important_parameters: Carrier mobility, temperature, and magnetic field strength.
main_assumptions: The nonlinear Hall conductivity is driven by the skew scattering of self-rotating electron wave packets.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the NLHE and the gap in current research regarding high-symmetry lattices, presents experimental observations in CoSi using crossbar devices, provides a theoretical framework involving the Magnus-like effect of wave packets, and analyzes the temperature and field dependence of the signal.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
